coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of aggregation-induced multi-emission and solid-state room-temperature-phosphorescence carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Hong, Dan
Xiang, Xian-yue
Zhou, Qiu-ju
Zhu, Yu-Lin
Wu, Yong-ping
Yang, Jun
Chen, Shi-hong
Tan, Ke-jun
coredata.dc:description: Multi-emission carbon dots (M-CDs) have broad applications. However, reports of the synthesis of M-CDs and their emission mechanism are limited. In this study, M-CDs with three emissions are synthesized by a one-pot solvothermal method, and the luminescence mechanism is investigated in detail. The prepared M-CDs show significant concentration-dependent properties. For high-concentration M-CDs (HC CDs), the surface-defect and carbon-core luminescence states are related to emission centers at 423 and 586 nm, respectively. The emission center at 493 nm are mainly due to the self-absorption of M-CDs. Conversely, low-concentration M-CDs (LC CDs) have 423 and 586 nm emissions. The blue CDs (B-CDs) and red CDs (R-CDs) are extracted from the prepared M-CD solution. The structural and optical properties indicate that the 493 nm peak is related to aggregation and self-absorption between B-CDs and R-CDs in HC CDs. Surprisingly, solid CDs (S-CDs) with solid-state room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) are simultaneously obtained during the preparation of the M-CDs. The mechanism investigation results show that S-CDs are composed of CDs and NaHCO3, and NaHCO3 acts as an oxygen barrier to prevent the quenching of the triplet and rigidly holds the luminescent guests to suppress vibrational dissipation. The M-CDs are successfully applied to detect Morin, while the S-CDs are demonstrated for optical anti-counterfeiting applications.

(A) Normalized FL spectra and (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of the M-CDs with different concentrations under an excitation wavelength of 365 nm (The concentrations 1 to 13 correspond to 0.027, 0.034, 0.068, 0.135, 0.169, 0.270, 0.338, 0.450, 0.540, 0.675, 0.900, 1.080, and 1.350 mg/mL). (C) Photographs of samples (left to right) 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 9, 10, and 13 under daylight and 365 nm UV light.

UV–vis absorption spectra of (A) the B-CDs and R-CDs. Inset: photographs of the B-CDs and R-CDs under daylight and a 365 nm UV lamp. (B) UV–vis absorption of the LC CDs (0.068 mg/mL), the mixture of the B-CDs and R-CDs, and the maximum excitation and emission spectra of the LC CDs. Inset: Photographs of the LC CDs in an aqueous solution under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). FL spectra of (C) the B-CDs and (D) R-CDs under different excitation wavelengths.

TEM images of the (A) B-CDs and (B) R-CDs. Inset: particle size distributions and lattice fringes. TEM images of high concentrations of the (C) B-CDs and (D) R-CDs. TEM images of the (E) LC CDs (F–H) M-CDs (1.350 mg/mL).
(A) XPS survey spectra of the M-CDs and the high-resolution narrow scan spectra of the (B) C 1s, (C) O 1s, and (D) N 1s bands. The colored areas indicate the deconvoluted peaks.
2D FL spectra of the (A) HC CDs and (B) LC CDs.

(A, C) FL spectra and (B, D) phosphorescence spectra of the S-CDs, S-CDs-1, PAN-S-CDs, and Ru-bpy-S-CDs at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm (A, B) and 254 nm (C, D).

Photographs of the S-CDs dispersed in DMF with various water contents (10–100 vol%, such as 100% means purely water solution) under daylight and 365 nm UV lamp illumination, and after turning the UV lamp off.

(A) FL emission spectra of the M-CDs with the addition of various concentrations of Morin. Inset: photographs of the M-CDs fluorescing in the presence of different concentrations of Morin under a 365 nm UV lamp. (B) Ratios of the emission intensities at 423 and 552 nm, (F′ 552/F′ 423)/(F 552/F 423) as a function of Morin concentration.

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of aggregation-induced multi-emission and solid-state room-temperature-phosphorescence carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multi-emission carbon dots (M-CDs) have broad applications. However, reports of the synthesis of M-CDs and their emission mechanism are limited. In this study, M-CDs with three emissions are synthesized by a one-pot solvothermal method, and the luminescence mechanism is investigated in detail. The prepared M-CDs show significant concentration-dependent properties. For high-concentration M-CDs (HC CDs), the surface-defect and carbon-core luminescence states are related to emission centers at 423 and 586 nm, respectively. The emission center at 493 nm are mainly due to the self-absorption of M-CDs. Conversely, low-concentration M-CDs (LC CDs) have 423 and 586 nm emissions. The blue CDs (B-CDs) and red CDs (R-CDs) are extracted from the prepared M-CD solution. The structural and optical properties indicate that the 493 nm peak is related to aggregation and self-absorption between B-CDs and R-CDs in HC CDs. Surprisingly, solid CDs (S-CDs) with solid-state room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) are simultaneously obtained during the preparation of the M-CDs. The mechanism investigation results show that S-CDs are composed of CDs and NaHCO3, and NaHCO3 acts as an oxygen barrier to prevent the quenching of the triplet and rigidly holds the luminescent guests to suppress vibrational dissipation. The M-CDs are successfully applied to detect Morin, while the S-CDs are demonstrated for optical anti-counterfeiting applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Since their discovery in carbon nanotubes in 2004 [1], carbon dots (CDs) have been widely used in many fields, such as bioimaging, cancer diagnosis and treatment, photocatalysis, and environmental science [2–5]. Owing to their tremendous advantages of low toxicity, optical stability, biocompatibility, and abundant precursor sources, CDs have attracted extensive research attention [6–9]. In particular, numerous studies have developed CDs with a wide excitation range, high quantum yield (QY) [10], and dual or multi-emissions [11,12]. However, compared with single-emission CDs, multi-emission CDs (M-CDs) are more challenging to obtain. Although multi-colored emissions can be obtained by changing the excitation wavelength [13–15] achieving multiple emission peaks for a single excitation wavelength is difficult. Therefore, further study on M-CDs is required.

In contrast to the reported CdSe quantum dots, CDs are not significantly affected by the quantum confinement effect [16], making it difficult to synthesize CDs with tunable fluorescence (FL) and long-wavelength emissions. The bandgap formation of CDs is mainly attributed to structural defects [17], as well as surface [18], molecular [19] and carbon-core luminescence states [20]. Song et al. [21] synthesized dual-emission CDs and proposed that the blue emission was caused by the graphite structure of sp 2 and defect transition of sp 3. Further different energy bandgaps can be introduced by heteroatom doping [22]. The origin of multi-emissions in CDs may be related to the particle-size effect and surface-state modification through solvent adjustments [17,23]. Chen et al. [13] have reported that CDs have multi-emission centers in dimethylformamide (DMF) solution, where DMF acts as a polar aprotic solvent that prevents the formation of non-radiative recombination in oxygen-related states, thereby resulting in various emission states. Furthermore, surface-state emission can be caused by a difference in electron distribution between the surface and bulk [24]. Distance-dependent intermolecular interactions can influence the surface electron distribution, where radiative recombination of electrons results in the formation of two or more emission centers [25]. Mu et al. [26] considered that the red-shift of the emission peak at high concentrations CDs was mainly due to intermolecular forces. With an increasing CDs concentration, the distance between the CDs decreases, and they aggregate because of the intermolecular forces, which change their surface state and result in longer-wavelength emission. Despite several reports of multi-emission centers induced by changing the CDs concentration, further research is required to clarify the mechanism. Here, we report a new aggregation-induced emission property of CDs. Concentration-dependent aggregation-induced luminescence is not caused by one type of CDs, but by the aggregation of two different CDs at high concentrations. The formed CDs have a new luminescence center, which is different from that of either CDs.

Room-temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials are widely used in photoelectric devices [27], sensors [28], and data security [29] because of their long lifetimes. However, the preparation of RTP materials remains challenging because of intersystem crossing and spin-forbidden properties of luminescent-materials, as well as non-radiative deactivation caused by intramolecular vibration and rotation. To date, several conventional preparation methods for RTP materials rely on the introduction of heavy metals, halogen bonds, carbonyl bonds, siloxy groups, or C−N/C=N bonds [30,31]. However, the low-efficiency spin-orbit coupling of pure organic molecules produces weak phosphorescence. Therefore, numerous researchers have embedded luminescent composites into an appropriate matrix [32] and crystal [33] to obtain efficient RTP materials. As a low-toxicity, high-efficiency luminescent material, CDs display phosphorescent behavior that has attracted considerable research attention. However, aggregation-induced quenching of CDs impedes the development of solid FL or phosphorescence. Several researchers have attempted to encapsulate CDs into a gel, such as polyvinylpyrrolidone and polyvinyl alcohol, to protect them from reacting with singlet oxygen, and thus to quench phosphorescence [34,35]. Furthermore, because of their complex and inefficient processes, CDs have limited practical applications at present.

In this study, we simultaneously synthesized M-CDs with three emission peaks in the visible region and CDs with solid-state RTP properties (S-CDs) using α-pyridylazo-β-naphthol (PAN) and tris(2,2-bipyridyl)ruthenium(II) chloride hexahydrate (Ru-bpy) as the carbon source by a one-pot solvothermal method. This strategy is summarized in Scheme 1 . The red and blue CDs (R-CDs and B-CDs, respectively) were obtained from M-CDs by extraction and separation, and concentration-dependent M-CDs were fabricated in-situ using R-CDs and B-CDs. Furthermore, to demonstrate their practical application, LC CDs were used for the ratio-fluorescence determination of Morin.

The prepared M-CDs exhibit concentration-dependent luminescence properties. Fig. 1 A shows the emission spectra of the M-CDs solution at a concentration of 0.900 mg/mL under different excitation wavelengths. Under all excitation wavelengths, this solution has three emission centers in the visible region located at approximately 423, 493, and 586 nm. When the concentration of the M-CDs decreases to 0.270 mg/mL (Fig. 1B), three emission centers remain under the excitation range of 325–375 nm, the peak at 493 nm becomes a shoulder peak, and the relative FL intensity of the red emission decreases. With a further decrease in the M-CDs concentration, the emission center at 493 nm gradually disappears (Fig. 1C and D). When changing the concentration from 0.900 to 0.068 mg/mL (Fig. 1A to D, respectively), the maximum excitation wavelength of the M-CDs changes from 375 to 305 nm, respectively, and the strongest emission changes from approximately 493 to 385 nm, respectively, indicating that the structure of the M-CDs may have changed. In contrast to those of traditional concentration-dependent CDs, the number of emission peaks of the M-CDs varies with concentration. Excessively large or small concentrations of the M-CDs are not conducive for subsequent research and applications. Therefore, HC CDs and LC CDs with concentrations of 0.270 and 0.068 mg/mL, respectively, were selected for further study. Both solutions of HC CDs and LC CDs show multiple emissions in the excitation wavelength range of 325–375 nm; therefore, the commonly used 365 nm excitation source was selected as the excitation wavelength for facilitating visualization.

The FL and absorption spectra at various M-CDs concentrations are shown in Fig. 2 A and B, respectively. Figure 2A shows the normalized FL spectra of the M-CDs at various concentrations. As the M-CDs concentration increases, the blue emission peak exhibits a blue shift (Δ = 4.00 nm), and a new emission peak appears at 493 nm. The FL intensity of the M-CDs first increases and then decreases with increasing M-CDs concentration (Fig. S1A) because of the self-quenching effect of the CDs [36]; this can be due to the aggregation of the CDs at high concentrations (0.270–1.350 mg/mL). As the concentration of the M-CDs increases, the green emission shifts to longer wavelengths (Fig. S1B). The correlation coefficient (R 2) between the green emission peak and concentration is 0.9986. No evident turning point is observed on the curve, indicating that the green emission is caused by one type of radiation [25]. One or more CDs may cause this radiation transition. Fig. 2B shows the absorbance of the M-CDs. The absorption intensities gradually increase with the concentration increase of M-CDs. When the concentration was too high, no obvious peak pattern could be seen in the absorption spectra. When M-CDs concentration exceeds 0.450 mg/mL, the color of M-CDs changes to dark red-brown under daylight ( Fig. 2C), indicating that reabsorption may occur in the M-CDs solution. To explore the origin of the M-CDs, we separated the M-CDs, as shown in S1 and Fig. S2 . Two types of CDs (B-CDs and R-CDs) were obtained with blue and red emissions, respectively. Based on the principle of similarity and compatibility, according to the different polarities of B-CDs and R-CDs, they can be well separated by ethyl acetate ( Figs. S2 and S1 ). Fig. S3 shows their absorbance spectra in low and high concentrations (their concentration is equal to M-CDs at a concentration of 1.350 mg/mL and 0.068 mg/mL, respectively). It is obvious that with the increase of concentration, the absorption intensity of B-CDs is stronger in the 400–600 nm wavelength ranges. And because the absorption value of R-CDs is too large at a high concentration, the absorption spectrum of R-CDs cannot be accurately judged.

The FL spectra of the separated R-CDs (Figure S4A). As the number of extractions increases, the red emission peak derived from the R-CDs becomes the largest emission peak, and the FL intensity of the green emission at 493 nm decreases with the removal of the B-CDs. In addition, the 2D FL spectra of the B-CDs at high concentrations were measured. As shown in Figure S4B, high concentrations of B-CDs have a green emission center in the excitation wavelength range of 430–490 nm and a blue emission center in the range of 280–420 nm. No multi-emission peaks were found under a single excitation wavelength, indicating that the green emission is not produced by the B-CDs at high concentrations.

The optical absorption spectra of the B-CDs and R-CDs are shown in Fig. 3 A. The absorption peak of the B-CDs at 280 nm is attributed to the π–π* electronic transition of the C=C bonds [37], and the bond at 330 nm is thought to originate from surface defect states that are mainly induced by the n–π* transitions of the C=O/C=N bonds [38]. The absorption spectrum of the R-CDs has three absorption peaks. The π–π* electronic transition produces absorption at 235 and 280 nm, while the third broad absorption band observed at 455 nm denotes the carboxyl-modified bipyridine ligands; this finding indicates that part of the Ru-bpy structure remains in the R-CDs [39]. The inset of Fig. 3A shows photographs of the B-CDs and R-CDs solutions under daylight and 365 nm UV light. Under daylight, the B-CDs solutions are transparent and mostly colorless, while the R-CDs solutions are light yellow, as is consistent with their absorption in the visible region. The absorption of the LC CDs solutions is similar to that of the mixed solution of the B-CDs and R-CDs (Fig. 3B), indicating that the interaction between the B-CDs and R-CDs at low concentrations is minimal. Fig. 3C shows the emission spectra of the B-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. When the excitation wavelength is 325 or 335 nm, dual emission appears at 385 and 410 nm. When the excitation wavelength increases, the emission peak at 385 nm decreases in intensity, while the other peak at 410 nm is red-shifted, indicating that there are two different luminescence states in the B-CDs. The R-CDs solution exhibits excitation-independent emission (Fig. 3D). The maximum excitation wavelength and absorption peak of the R-CDs solution are approximately 280 nm, which is attributed to the π–π* transition of the sp 2 carbogenic domains [13].

Fig. 4 A and B show the TEM images of the B-CDs and R-CDs, respectively. The R-CDs and B-CDs are observed as uniformly distributed nanoparticles with average diameters of 3.38 and 2.31 nm, respectively. The insets of Fig. 4A and B show the lattice fringes of the B-CDs with a spacing of 0.25 and 0.26 nm and those of R-CDs with a spacing of 0.21 and 0.29 nm, respectively. Compared with those of the B-CDs, the lattice fringes of the R-CDs are more pronounced, whereas most of the B-CDs are amorphous but with some degree of crystallization, indicating that the R-CDs have a higher degree of graphitization. The Raman spectra ( Fig. S5 ) of them still proved this. The D band at approximately 1350 cm −1 is attributed to the functional groups on the CDs’ surface and indicates hydrocarbon or aliphatic moieties connected to the graphitic structure. [40] The G band, which appears around 1580 cm −1 , corresponds to in-place vibrations and shows the presence of small graphitic planes or microcrystallites. The intensity ratios (I D /I G ) of the two bands are 0.88 and 0.65, respectively, indicating that there were more structural defects in B-CDs than in R-CDs. The TEM images of the B-CDs and R-CDs at high concentrations are shown in Fig. 4C and D, respectively. At higher concentrations, the particle sizes of the B-CDs and R-CDs are much larger, and the B-CDs form a “chain-like polymer”. The inset HRTEM image in Fig. 4D shows that the R-CDs have lamellar nanocrystals. In addition, the TEM images of the M-CDs at different concentrations were obtained to observe changes in the particle size of the M-CDs. As shown in Fig. 4E, the LC CDs are uniformly dispersed with an average particle size of 2.81 nm. However, when the concentration increases to 1.350 mg/mL, aggregation occurs, and the size significantly increases to 59.58 nm (Fig. 4F). Fig. 4G and H of this same sample clearly show aggregation. In addition, the results of particle size analysis show that the size of M-CDs increases gradually with the concentration increases of M-CDs, which also indicating the aggregation of M-CDs at high concentrations (Fig. S6). The XRD pattern of the M-CDs (Fig. S7A) shows a weak broad peak at 2θ = 16–45° related to the π–π stacking of the carbon structure of the M-CDs. The peaks at 12° and 45.8° originate from the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [41], while that at 21.5° is the closest to the (001) facets of graphitic carbon [42]. The results show that the obtained M-CDs are mainly graphitic.

Considering that B-CDs and R-CDs aggregate at high concentrations, we measured the FL spectra of different concentrations of the B-CDs and R-CDs at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. As shown in Fig. S7B, the B-CDs and R-CDs have blue and red emission peaks, respectively, and their FL intensities depend on the concentration of the CDs solution. However, the FL spectra of the B-CDs and R-CDs at low or high concentrations do not show a new emission peak at approximately 493 nm, and the emission spectra do not overlap. Therefore, the new emission at 493 nm is not produced by the self-quenching of the B-CDs or R-CDs but by the interactions between them, thereby confirming that the concentration-dependent optical properties of the M-CDs are related to the aggregation of the B-CDs and R-CDs. The resulting aggregates retain the emissions of the R-CDs and B-CDs and also produce a new emission peak [43].

To study the function of the raw materials in the preparation of the M-CDs, we synthesized the PAN-CDs and Ru-bpy-CDs using only PAN or Ru-bpy, respectively. The 2D FL spectra of the B-CDs, R-CDs, PAN-CDs, and Ru-bpy-CDs are shown in Fig. S8. The B-CDs and PAN-CDs exhibit similar optical-emission behavior with excitation-independent luminescence and similar FL spectra. Considering the high similarity between the synthetic raw materials of the two CDs, we speculated that the CDs might have the same surface functional groups. A similar hypothesis was proposed for R-CDs and Ru-bpy-CDs. Therefore, we believe that the other raw materials minimally interfere during B-CDs and R-CDs preparations. The B-CDs are mainly obtained from the hydroxyl-rich raw carbon source, PAN, while the R-CDs are formed from Ru-bpy with large conjugate planes, forming M-CDs at high concentrations.

The zeta potentials of the B-CDs, R-CDs, and LC CDs were further measured (Fig. S9A) to study the interaction between the R-CDs and B-CDs. The results show that the surface charges of the B-CDs, R-CDs, and LC CDs are negative with zeta potentials of −43.33, −7.92, and −15.33 mV, respectively. The zeta potential of the LC CDs is between that of B-CDs and R-CDs, indicating an electrostatic force between the B-CDs and R-CDs. However, no new emission peak is observed at this concentration, indicating that the electrostatic force is not the main reason for the new emission peak. The zeta potentials of the different concentrations of the M-CDs are shown in Fig. S9B. The zeta potentials increase from −16.87 mV to −10.08 mV with an increasing M-CD concentration from 0.027 mg/mL to 1.350 mg/mL; the absolute value of the zeta potential gradually decreases, indicating that the M-CDs prefer to agglomerate at high concentrations [44].

The chemical properties of the M-CDs were evaluated using XPS and FTIR. The XPS survey scan spectrum (Fig. 5 A) shows peaks at 284.8, 399.0, 462.3, and 532.0 eV, corresponding to C 1s, N 1s, Ru 3p, and O 1s, respectively. The atomic contents of C/N/O/Ru are 84.79%, 1.89%, 0.41%, and 12.91%, respectively. The C 1s spectrum is composed of C–C/C=N (284.65 eV), amorphous C (285.1 eV), C–O (285.6 eV), C=O (288.4 eV), and O–C=O (290.2 eV) (Fig. 5B) [45,46]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 5C) is resolved to C=O (531.2 eV), C–O (532.2 eV), O–C–O (533.2 eV), and O–H (535.6 eV) [3,47], respectively, which are related to the carboxyl acid groups. The N 1s peaks (Fig. 5D) at 398.9, 399.8, and 400.7 eV confirm the existence of the pyridine/type N atoms, pyrrolic type N atoms, and N–H bonds, respectively [48,49]. Thus, many functional groups are present on the surface of the M-CDs, such as –OH, –NH, and –COOH. Moreover, amino N indicates that Ru-bpy has undergone a certain degree of carbonization during M-CDs formation. Fig. S10A shows the FTIR spectra of the M-CDs with different concentrations; the stretching vibrations of the C–O, C–N, and C=O/C=C bonds are located at 1113, 1381, and 1640 cm−1, respectively [50,51]. With an increase in the M-CDs concentration, the peak related to the O–H bond at 3498 cm−1 shifts to 3433 cm−1, indicating that hydrogen bonds are formed between the functional groups on the surface of the M-CDs [52]. When the concentration increases to 1.350 mg/mL, the absorption peak at 3070 cm−1 is attributed to the amide association state. By contrast, the absorption vibration peak at 1516 cm−1 weakens, indicating that the free amide disappears. Hydrogen bonds play an important role in the aggregation of the M-CDs. The FTIR spectra of the low concentration B-CDs and R-CDs samples (Fig. S10B) indicate the presence of O–H and N–H bonds. As the concentration of the M-CDs increases, the association state of the hydrogen bonds possibly appears because of the aggregation of the B-CDs and R-CDs. A green emission peak is observed for the HC CDs solution (Fig. 1), which may be related to the hydrogen bond force between the B-CDs and R-CDs.

The 2D FL spectra of the HC CDs and LC CDs are shown in Fig. 6 . The HC CDs have four emission centers over the entire spectrum (centers 1, 2, 3, and 4 are located at 385, 423, 493, and 586 nm, respectively). The LC CDs only have two emission centers because their blue and red emissions are too strong and too weak, respectively, to be visible in the 2D FL spectrum (Fig. 6B). The LC CDs show two emission peaks under the excitation wavelength of 365 nm (Fig. S11). Center 1 has excitation-independent luminescence properties and a QY of up to 24.80% (Table S2), which can be attributed to molecular-state luminescence [53].

Further, Fig. S12A shows the pH-sensing ability of the LC CDs. With an increase in pH from 4.41 to 11.96, the FL intensity of the peak at 423 nm increases, while that at 586 nm remains stable. The intensity ratio F 423/F 586 as a function of pH (inset of Fig. S12A) shows a smooth curve in the pH range of 4.41–11.96, showing a high R 2 when fit into a polynomial relationship. The changes in F 423/F 586 with pH are attributed to the protonation and deprotonation of the hydroxyl groups on the surface of the B-CDs. The stabilities of the FL intensity over time of the LC and HC CDs are shown in Figs. S12B and S12C, respectively. The FL intensity of the blue emission rapidly and sharply declines under continuous irradiation at 365 nm with a Xe lamp (Power: 500 W) for 30 min, whereas the FL intensity of the HC CDs exhibits better photostability than the LC CDs. These outcomes may be because the –OH and –NH groups on the surface of the HC CDs tend to form hydrogen bonds at high concentrations, thereby improving structural rigidity and optical stability [45]. Based on these experimental results, Center 2 has excitation-dependent luminescence characteristics and is easily affected by environmental factors such as temperature, pH, and solvent. Thus, this center is regarded as the surface-state luminescence center, which is produced by the surface functional groups of the M-CDs [53,54]. The red emission peak at 586 nm does not change with the excitation wavelength and is not significantly affected by environmental factors. The molecular-state luminescence always has a higher QY than the carbon-core luminescence state. The carbon-core luminescence state has high stability and low FL intensity [19,55] The QY of Center 4 is 3.80% (Table S2), which is lower than that of center 1. Therefore, center 4 is attributed to the characteristics of the sp 2 carbogenic domains, which are related to the carbon-core luminescence state.

The self-absorption of B-CDs may also lead to the three emission peaks of M-CDs [57]. The absorption spectra of B-CDs (Fig. S3) at the high concentration show a relatively broad absorption peak between 400 and 600 nm. Combined with the 2D FL spectrum of B-CDs (Fig. S4B) in the high concentration, it can be seen that B-CDs are in the excitation range of 420–550 nm, and the emission center is about 500 nm. The high concentration of B-CDs only has one emission peak under a single excitation because the luminous blue center is too strong, thus covering the luminous center at 500 nm. After the addition of R-CDs, due to the FRET between the two CDs, the FL intensity of blue generation is further reduced, making the green FL peak produced by self-absorption appear. The relevant mechanism diagram is shown in Fig. 7 .

S-CDs with solid-state RTP were obtained during the preparation of the M-CDs. To investigate the origin of the solid-state RTP, the S-CDs-1 (The synthesis process of the S-CDs-1 was the same as that used to prepare the S-CDs, except for the absence of NaOH), PAN-S-CDs, and Ru-bpy-S-CDs were also prepared. As shown in Fig. 8 , the S-CDs and PAN-S-CDs show a yellow emission denoting the solid-state RTP, while Ru-bpy-S-CDs show red FL and virtually no solid-state RTP under 365 nm excitation using a UV lamp. Under the excitation of a 254 nm UV lamp, the S-CDs, PAN-S-CDs, and Ru-bpy-CDs show green, yellow, and blue solid RTP, respectively. Furthermore, S-CDs-1 shows no solid-state RTP under either UV lamp. The observed solid-state RTP of the S-CDs is longer than that of the other CDs. Notably, the FL and phosphorescence emission of the S-CDs are similar to those of the PAN-S-CDs but are different from those of the Ru-bpy-S-CDs. These findings may be explained as follows: similar to the M-CDs, S-CDs are composed of PAN-S-CDs and Ru-bpy-S-CDs; owing to the excessively strong emission of the PAN-S-CDs, which masks the FL or phosphorescence of the Ru-bpy-S-CDs, the existence of the Ru-bpy-S-CDs still affects the emission of the S-CDs.

Fig. 9 A and B show the FL and phosphorescence spectra of the S-CDs under 365 nm excitation, respectively. The Ru-bpy-S-CDs have two FL emission centers located at 425 and 600 nm, the latter of which contributes red FL under an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. Although the phosphorescence spectra of the Ru-bpy-S-CDs show solid-state RTP, the phosphorescence intensity is too weak to be observed by the naked eye Fig. 8). The FL and phosphorescence spectra of the same samples under 254 nm excitation are shown in Fig. 9C and D, respectively. The Ru-bpy-S-CDs have the largest phosphorescence intensity with the luminescence center located at 438 nm under 254 nm excitation wavelength. The solid-state RTP of the S-CDs is dependent on the excitation wavelength. Fig. S14 shows the normalized FL and phosphorescence spectra of the S-CDs under 254 or 365 nm excitation, respectively. Under 254 and 365 nm excitation, the Stokes shifts of the S-CDs between FL and RTP emission are 91 and 61 nm, corresponding to the singlet-triplet energy levels at 0.45 and 0.32 eV, respectively. The large Stokes shifts are attributed to energy losses due to the multiple non-radiative transitions from the first excited singlet state to the triplet state [33]. The phosphorescence peaks of the S-CDs at excitation wavelengths of 254 and 365 nm are 546 and 516 nm, respectively, with RTP colors of green and yellow, respectively. This result may be due to the red-shift in phosphorescence under the 254 nm-excitation and the higher peak intensity than that observed under 365 nm excitation [58]. The FL and phosphorescence spectra of S-CDs-1 show no FL or solid-state RTP under 254 and 365 nm excitation (Fig. 9). To understand the origin of the solid RTP, XRD spectra were recorded (Fig. S15). The crystal phases of the S-CDs, PAN-S-CDs, and Ru-bpy-S-CDs are similar to those of NaHCO3, whereas S-CDs-1 shows a significantly different pattern, indicating that NaHCO3 is significant to solid-state RTP. Because the S-CDs show the best phosphorescence properties of all the investigated samples, they were used for subsequent experiments to explore the solid-state-RTP mechanism. The phosphorescence lifetimes of the S-CDs under the excitation wavelengths of 254 and 365 nm are 28.7 and 4.43 μs, respectively (Fig. S16), which are similar to those obtained for ionic salts [59].

The S-CDs were dissolved in DMF and water solvents to investigate their RTP properties. As shown in Fig. 10 , the S-CDs dissolve well in water solvent; however, with decreasing water content, the S-CDs disperse in the mixed water–DMF solution. Moreover, the S-CDs solution shows weak blue luminescence under the excitation of a 365 nm UV lamp. The S-CDs solution is opaque when the water content is ≤ 40 vol% and transparent with higher water contents, indicating that the S-CDs separate from the solution at low water contents. The RTP of the aqueous S-CDs solutions is only observed for water contents ≤40 vol%, indicating that the solid state is crucial for the RTP behavior of the S-CDs.

The TEM images in Fig. S17 show that the S-CDs are spherical and well dispersed in a water solution, with an average particle size of 2.2 nm and a clear lattice fringe spacing of 0.29 nm (inset Fig. S17B) attributed to the graphite (002) plane corresponding to the diffraction facets of the graphene [60]. As shown in Fig. S17A, considering the previous XRD results, the CDs are encapsulated in a crystal structure, possibly NaHCO3. To confirm this hypothesis, we obtained the XPS and FTIR spectra of the S-CDs. The XPS survey spectra (Fig. S18) show that the S-CDs are rich in Na. Because the S-CDs have no RTP when dissolved in water (WS-CDs), the FTIR spectra of the S-CDs and WS-CDs are compared (Fig. S19). The FTIR spectrum of the S-CDs shows the characteristic peaks of NaHCO3 at 1390, 1300, 1005, 839, and 680 cm−1, which are not present in the spectrum of the WS-CDs sample; these results indicate that the S-CDs structure is destroyed in the aqueous solution. Considering the preparation method and raw materials, the formation of NaHCO3 possibly results from the easy decomposition of DMF into sodium formate and dimethylamine at high temperatures in an alkaline medium. Then, sodium formate is decomposed into ammonium oxalate and converted into sodium bicarbonate at high temperatures [61]. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA; Fig. S20) of the S-CDs shows only one major weight-loss region in the range of 150–270 °C, which is consistent with the loss of NaHCO3. Therefore, solid-state RTP in the S-CDs results from NaHCO3 acting as an oxygen barrier to prevent the quenching of the triplet state, thereby inhibiting the aggregation-induced quenching effect of the CDs and suppressing the vibrational energy dissipation [62]. Taking advantage of these properties, S-CDs could be developed as promising encryption materials [63,64]. As shown in Fig. 11 A, S-CDs can be applied to anti-counterfeiting because they easily dissolve in water, their F remains unchanged, and phosphorescence disappears. In the real application process, the S-CDs can be directly applied based on the sensitivity of the materials to water; thus, the information can be encrypted. The digital 8085 is encrypted by using a fluorescent pen in the same color as the S-CDs (Fig. 11B). Further, by utilizing the different FL and phosphorescence properties of the S-CDs, PAN-S-CDs, and Ru-bpy-S-CDs, we can achieve image time anti-counterfeiting. As shown in Fig. 11E, a flower is prepared using three phosphorescent materials, with the petal, pedicle, and leaf prepared using the Ru-bpy-S-CDs, PAN-S-CDs, and S-CDs, respectively. Under the irradiation of a 365 nm UV lamp, the flowers appear red. After turning off the UV lamp, the flowers turn blue. With time, the blue stems and blue flowers gradually disappear, leaving only green leaves. The S-CDs show good optical time anti-counterfeiting performance and can be applied to complex anti-counterfeiting environments [65].

To demonstrate the potential application of the M-CDs, we used them as a fluorescent probe to detect Morin. Morin is an essential flavonoid that is widespread in plants and plant-derived foods. It exhibits many biological activities, such as anti-oxidation, anti-mutagenesis, anti-inflammation, and anti-virulence [66,67]. The current methods for detecting Morin require special equipment and complicated processes. Thus, a simple, reliable, and reproducible analytical method for detecting Morin would be of significant value. Based on the internal filtering effect, Morin was detected using an FL ratiometric method in this study. The FL intensity of the LC CDs at 423 nm decreases, while a new FL peak appears at 552 nm with the addition of Morin (Fig. 12 A). The ratio (F′ 552/F′ 423)/(F 552/F 423) are evaluated, where F′ and F are the FL intensities of the LC CDs with and without Morin, respectively, and the subscripts refer to the emission wavelength. A linear relationship between the logarithm of this ratio and Morin concentration in the range of 0.5–200 μmol/L is observed, with a limit of detection of 0.21 μmol/L (Fig. 12B). The obtained linear regression equations are given in Fig. 12B, with R 2 values > 0.99. The optimization, selectivity, and interference mechanisms are discussed in sections S3, S4 and S5, of the supplementary information, respectively.

Furthermore, human urine and serum samples were used as real samples for Morin detection. The human urine and serum samples were spiked with three Morin concentrations, and the analytical results are listed in Table S4. The recoveries of Morin in human urine and serum samples are in the ranges of 90.02–93.36% and 92.22–98.20%, respectively. The relative standard deviation is less than 2.4%. The results indicate that the ratiometric FL method based on the M-CDs is feasible for practical sample determination.

M-CDs were successfully fabricated based on the concentration-dependent properties of the constituent B-CDs and R-CDs. Under a single excitation wavelength, the prepared M-CDs showed blue, green, and red emissions in the visible region at high concentrations, whereas only red and blue emission peaks were observed at low concentrations. Unlike previously reported concentration-dependent luminescence properties based on a red or blue shift of the emission peaks, the M-CDs also showed a new emission peak. With increasing M-CDs concentration, the B-CDs and R-CDs showed a tricolor emission band from the blue to red region, which covered most of the visible range. Moreover, M-CDs can be constructed using white light-emitting devices. At a low M-CDs concentration, based on the internal filtration effect between the M-CDs and Morin, the FL color changed from pink to purple to green, thereby achieving the semi-quantitative visual detection of Morin by the naked eye. Furthermore, the S-CDs with solid-state phosphorescence were obtained, which could provide a security protection strategy for high-level information and potential applications in optical anti-counterfeiting materials.

Here, α-pyridylazo-β-naphthol (PAN, 90%) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd.(Shanghai, China); tris(2, 2-bipyridyl) ruthenium (II) chloride hexahydrate (Ru-bpy, 98%) and NaOH were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.(Shanghai, China), DMF was purchased from Chongqing Titanium New Chemical Co., Ltd. (Chongqing, China) and Morin (C15H10O7) was purchased from Shanghai Maikun Chemical Co., Ltd.(Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water (18.2 Ω cm, Milli-Q) was used in the experiment.

FL spectra were measured using an F-7000 FL spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), and UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using a UV-2500 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Infrared spectra (FT-IR-8400S) were obtained using a Shimadzu Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a Thermo Escalab 250 Xi X-ray electron spectrometer (Somerfly Technology, USA). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) assignation of phases was obtained by an X-ray powder diffractometer (BRUKER D8 ADVANCE, Bruker and Germany). FL and phosphorescence lifetime measurements were performed using an FL-S1000 steady/transient FL spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK) and FS5 steady/transient FL spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK), respectively. The FL QYs were measured by absolute PL quantum yield spectrometer C11347 (Hamamatsu, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed using a Talos F200X (Somerfly, USA). Zeta potentials and hydrated particle sizes were measured using a Zetasizer Nano-ZS System (Malvern, English).

The high nitrogen content CDs often emit red wavelengths [68,69]. Ru-bpy, the raw material selected, can introduce red emissions and provide a large sp 2 conjugated structure for the CDs. Moreover, PAN can emit blue, which is related to the surface-state luminescence [22]. Therefore, the CDs synthesized using Ru-bpy and PAN may have multiple emissions.

A typical process for synthesizing a mixture of M-CDs and S-CDs was as follows. First, PAN (58.10 mg), Ru-bpy (20.10 mg), H2O (200 μL), 2 mol/L NaOH (200 μL), and DMF (4.60 mL) were placed in a Teflon reactor and subjected to solvothermal treatment at 200 °C for 7 h. After being cooled to room temperature, the S-CDs and M-CDs were separated by centrifugation. The supernatant contained M-CDs, and the sediment contained S-CDs. M-CDs were collected by diluting the supernatant five times with ultrapure water, centrifuging at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, filtering the reddish-brown supernatant through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane, and then drying in a vacuum drying chamber. The M-CD sample contained both B-CDs and R-CDs. The S-CDs were ultrasonically washed with DMF three times, and the canary yellow solid was dried entirely in a vacuum dryer.

To investigate the effect of PAN and Ru-bpy on the formation of the CDs, we synthesized the PAN-CDs and PAN-S-CDs using only PAN, while the Ru-bpy-CDs and Ru-bpy-S-CDs were fabricated using only Ru-bpy (Table S1). Other synthesis conditions and separation methods were the same as those described above. The synthesis process of the S-CDs-1 was the same as that used to prepare the S-CDs, except for the absence of NaOH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of morin and real-sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the practicability of the M-CDs, we used LC CDs to establish a ratiometric FL method for detecting Morin. First, H3BO4–KCl–Na2CO3 buffer solution (0.10 mL) and 0.675 mg/mL M-CDs (0.20 mL) were added to Eppendorf tubes (2 mL). Then, Morin solution (1 mmol/L) was added in amoun ts of 0–200 μL to the above mixture, which was then diluted to 1 mL with ultrapure water and thoroughly mixed. The FL spectra were recorded with 10 nm excitation and an emission slit at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The working standard solutions were prepared by diluting the stock solutions.

Further, to verify the method for practical applications, urine and human serum samples were obtained from healthy volunteers. The urine sample was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min. The human serum was stored at room temperature for 1 h and then overnight at 4 °C. The supernatant was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min, and the final supernatant was stored at 4 °C. For analysis, appropriate Morin standard solutions were spiked into the treated urine and human serum samples. All experiments were performed in compliance with relevant laws and guidelines.
